column8:
I have a lot to say about this, but first of all, I’m just so sad for the thousands of scientists who have dedicated decades of their lives to this field, yet will never fulfil their dream of getting into space—while a bunch of millionaires fly up there with absolutely no real purpose. There are scientists who dream every day of what these people just experienced in 11 minutes of flight.
Yes, maybe these women dreamt about it too, but none of them have worked towards this specific dream in the way scientists have—training, researching, and dedicating their lives to it.
On top of that, they’re now taking away records like “first all-female crew to…” or “first Vietnamese woman in space” (and I’m sorry if these aren’t factually correct—I haven’t finished researching yet…), which could’ve perfectly been achieved by hardworking women in the field who truly dedicated their lives to space exploration.
Don’t get me wrong—I’d never want to undermine the work these women have done in their own fields. I actually don’t know much about their careers since my interests lie elsewhere, but I’m sure they’re very talented and have contributed meaningfully to the world. I’m an Ariana fan, so I understand how much good artists can do.
But this mission was honestly just so painful to watch. And that’s not even getting into the issue of people around the world who are starving and dying. Even just thinking about people who work hard every day to reduce their carbon footprint by a bit—meanwhile, these women polluted more in 10 minutes than 30 people would in their lifetimes.
Finally, before some of you come for me, I’m not criticising the ‘all female group’, I am criticising any commercial flights to space (Any mission with scientific purpose is absolutely acceptable for me - this is my opinion).

column8: As a naturopath specializing in thyroid and gut health, I believe this conversation needs more nuance. While salts like Redmond, Celtic, and Himalayan do contain trace amounts of naturally occurring elements like lead, they also provide a rich spectrum of minerals essential for electrolyte balance and healing—especially for those recovering from chronic illness. There are many products that offer health benefits that have unavoidable levels of certain toxins, it’s why it is wise for many to consider including a binder (such as takesumi supreme) as part of a daily supplement stack. Notably, Redmond Real Salt is sourced from an ancient seabed, making it free from modern pollutants like microplastics . In contrast, salts such as David’s or Diamond Crystal are highly refined, often stripped of beneficial minerals, and may contain anti-caking agents that can be disruptive to sensitive systems. Maldon is a cleaner, additive-free option with a beautiful flake texture, but it lacks the mineral richness found in unrefined salts. For those seeking both purity and mineral density, Makai Pure from Selina Naturally is an excellent choice, tested free of detectable lead. Choosing salt isn’t just about what to avoid—it’s about selecting options that support the body’s healing processes.

column8:
### **0. The Fool** 
**Theme:** Beginnings, innocence, trust in nature. 
**Application:** Embracing veganism as a leap of faith into a compassionate lifestyle. The Fool’s curiosity mirrors the openness needed to question societal norms around animal use and reconnect with the natural world as stewards, not exploiters.

---

### **1. The Magician** 
**Theme:** Resourcefulness, manifestation, power. 
**Application:** Harnessing plant-based abundance to create sustainable solutions. The Magician challenges the myth that animal products are necessary, emphasizing humanity’s ability to innovate ethical alternatives (e.g., foraging local wild edibles, veganic forest gardening).

---

### **2. The High Priestess** 
**Theme:** Intuition, hidden knowledge, mystery. 
**Application:** Listening to the silent suffering of animals and the ecological costs of animal agriculture. The High Priestess urges us to trust our inner moral compass, revealing the unseen consequences of exploitation.

Colossal did NOT use ANY Aenocyon Dirus
DNA to make these pups. (Even though frozen mummified DNA does exist)
•They used Modern Grey Wolves (not even closely related to Aenocyon Dirus, of which the closest living relative is the African Jackal, not American grey wolf)
• Colossal simply edited a few genes in the gray wolves to give them traits they wanted- Larger size, wider skull, more muscle, and white coats. 
TL;DR: Colossal made designer modern grey wolves and is passing them off as the modern Game of Thrones "dire wolves"® to the masses, not the prehistoric kind that existed 13000years ago. Don't let this corporation gaslight you into thinking they've done something they absolutely did not, regardless of the influencer paid to say otherwise.
If someone edited chimpanzee DNA to make them taller, and half less body hair, and implanted the embryos in a gorilla to birth them as a surrogate (Colossal used large hounds to birth the “dire wolves” via cesarean) ….you wouldn’t call the hairless chimpanzee a “human”. So Don’t call these canines dire anything, except dire efforts to make the public more accepting of the CIA being among the corporation’s investors interested in their gene editing (Google it) and drumming up more $$$. 

If you're reading this, for the sake of objective scientific truth, please correct every post you see spreading misinformation.

column8:
"Stem is harder" 
"Nooo humanities are harder" 
How about you actually choose a degree you want to pursue? If you wanna pursue STEM simply because it's harder, more appreciated in society, higher paying and you want to talk about how smart you are, how hard it is etc, without actually having any interest in STEM, then your entire degree and effort put into it is pointless. Same goes for all degrees. It's okay to sometimes talk about how difficult your degree is, but if that's all you can talk about, I question the integrity of your choice in pursuit of the degree. Let's not undervalue degrees because they're more "feminine" or "easier". Even if it's hard for you to see the point of a certain degree or job, it doesn't mean it's pointless,  it just means you got to broaden your perspective and relate to people choosing what they love, not what society wants from them.

My degree is not silly.
